explicit_text: I enlisted in the service during World War II in April 1944 and served until June 1946. The war shaped many of my decisions, including leaving high school early to prepare for college and military service. I was aware of the conflict from the attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941, which deeply influenced my family and me. My service included training and preparation for officer roles as the war progressed, especially in the Pacific theater.

explicit_text: I vividly remember being at a basketball game with my father on December 7th, 1941, when the game was stopped to announce that Pearl Harbor had been bombed. At that moment, I asked my dad where Pearl Harbor was, and even he didn't know. That attack was a turning point that prepared me and my family for the reality of going into the service.

explicit_text: I attended St. Philip High School in Chicago, graduating in 1944. The school was taught by the Servite Brothers and Viatorian Fathers, many of whom became priests. Although the school closed in the late 1960s, it still holds reunions, and I continue to attend them despite fewer classmates showing up each year.

explicit_text: I began my Navy officer training at DePauw University in Greencastle, Indiana, where we took boot camp, physical education, and academic courses. The program was intense, with three trimesters per year, and after two years we would become midshipmen. After the war, I returned to DePauw to complete my degree under a special program for returning veterans.
